🧠 Self-Learning Engine — What the Model Has Learned

How this project works

A self-learning World Cup prediction system — model, backend, and live data pipeline.

The model

  • A random-forest classifier predicts home / draw / away from ~50k historical internationals; a Poisson regression estimates expected goals (xG) per side.
  • A Dixon-Coles matrix converts xG into full scoreline probabilities.
  • Prediction factors: loop-learned ELO, squad quality, star-player form, tactics, injuries/suspensions and news sentiment.

The learning loop

  • After every final whistle the backend re-trains: ELO ratings update (K=60 World Cup weight), online-learned xG biases shift, and every remaining prediction is recomputed.
  • An accuracy tracker scores every prediction against the real result — nothing is hidden or retro-fitted.
  • The corners model and goalscorer picks are walk-forward backtested: each pick uses only data available before that match.

The live backend

  • serve.py polls ESPN every 3 seconds during matches, writes goals, penalties and shootouts into the schedule, resolves bracket slots, and pushes SSE live updates into open browsers.
  • Missed results self-heal: a backfill pass re-fetches by date at boot and hourly, and a verify pass re-checks every recorded score against ESPN.
  • A GitHub Actions cron mirrors the same pipeline in CI, keeping the hosted page current around the clock.

Stack

Python · scikit-learn · SciPy · pandas · vanilla JS (zero front-end dependencies) · GitHub Actions · Render
